coredata.dc:title: Influence of Al contents on alpha-case formation of Ti–xAl alloys
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2005 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Sung, Si-Young
Kim, Young-Jig
coredata.dc:description: Alpha-case (α-case) formations between Al2O3 mold and Ti–xAl alloys were investigated for the economic Ti–xAl alloys net-shape forming. The α-case formation reaction was examined in a plasma arc melting furnace. The α-case reactions between mold and Ti–xAl alloys were characterized by optical microscopy and a hardness tester. When the Al concentration of Ti–xAl alloys was more than 30at.%, there were no α-case formation reactions. For the clear examination of the phenomenon, its α-case formation mechanism was elucidated by an electron probe micro-analyzer, transmission electron microscopy, and thermodynamic calculations. The α-case was formed not only by interstitial oxygen elements but also by substitutional metallic elements dissolved from mold materials. Therefore, in the case of more than 30at.% Ti–xAl alloys, there were neither interstitial nor substitutional α-case formations since Ti–xAl alloys have both negligible solubility of oxygen and low activity in molten states.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Titanium aluminides
Investment casting
Alpha-case
Net-shape forming

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of drop casting procedure with a plasma arc melting furnace.
Photographs of metal–mold interfacial region of as-cast: (a) cp Ti, (b) Ti–10Al, (c) Ti–30Al, (d) Ti–40Al, (e) Ti–47Al, and (f) Ti–55at.% Al alloys poured into Al2O3 mold.
Hardness profiles on the interfacial region of as-cast Ti–xAl alloys poured into Al2O3 mold.
Comparison of elemental mapping images of O, Al, and Si in Ti castings into Al2O3 mold and BEI image by EPMA.

Results of analytical transmission electron microscopy. (a) Cross-sectional bright field TEM image of the α-case region, (b) ring and spot patterns on the TEM image without C2 aperture, and (c) spot pattern on the TEM image was HCP phase in the [2ii0] beam direction.

Convergent beam electron diffraction analysis with primitive unit cell volume method. (a) The measured primitive unit cell volume, 61.08ang3 (CL=521.4mm, D 1 = D 2 =6.5mm, ANG=60°, CRAD=23mm, λ =0.0251Å), and (b) EDS spot analysis result.

article.ce:title: Influence of Al contents on alpha-case formation of Ti–xAl alloys

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Advanced Materials Engineering, Sungkyunkwan University, 300 Chunchun-dong, Jangan-gu, Suwon, Gyeonggi-do 440-746, South Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Alpha-case (α-case) formations between Al2O3 mold and Ti–xAl alloys were investigated for the economic Ti–xAl alloys net-shape forming. The α-case formation reaction was examined in a plasma arc melting furnace. The α-case reactions between mold and Ti–xAl alloys were characterized by optical microscopy and a hardness tester. When the Al concentration of Ti–xAl alloys was more than 30at.%, there were no α-case formation reactions. For the clear examination of the phenomenon, its α-case formation mechanism was elucidated by an electron probe micro-analyzer, transmission electron microscopy, and thermodynamic calculations. The α-case was formed not only by interstitial oxygen elements but also by substitutional metallic elements dissolved from mold materials. Therefore, in the case of more than 30at.% Ti–xAl alloys, there were neither interstitial nor substitutional α-case formations since Ti–xAl alloys have both negligible solubility of oxygen and low activity in molten states.

ce:section.ce:para: Ti3Al and TiAl are of great importance ranging from sports equipment to the power generation and chemical processing industries, automotive, and aerospace engineering [1,2]. A large number of researchers have been trying Ti–xAl alloys in an attempt to combine most of their advantages, such as high specific yield strength, good structural stability, and excellent resistance against oxidation and corrosion [3,4]. However, the practical utilization of Ti–xAl alloys have been limited since their forming processes are much more laborious than one may think, which is mainly due to the fact that because in solid solution Ti combines with carbon, nitrogen, and especially oxygen, Ti–xAl alloys are constrained to be melted in inert environments or in a vacuum [5]. In the early days, the development of Ti–xAl alloys had been focused mainly on the defense system technologies, thus their performance was a more crucial factor than the cost. However, the cost problem cannot be overlooked any more, especially in the field of automotive engineering. For the intended applications, Ti–xAl alloys forming processes should incorporate forging, powder metallurgy (PM), and casting. In the case of forging, it is difficult to apply the hot forging process to Ti–xAl alloys due to their inherent poor deformability and extremely high machining losses [6,7]. Besides, PM process has been limited by the freedom of size and shape, and soundness [8,9]. In Ti–xAl alloys casting, the unacceptable surface reaction layer called ‘alpha-case (α-case)’ occurs due to the high reactivity of Ti–xAl alloys in molten states [5]. This α-case on the Ti–xAl alloys castings must be removed by chemical milling since the α-case makes it difficult to machine them and can lead to crack initiation and propagation. Furthermore, the chemical milling process causes environmental pollution, the lowering of dimensional accuracy and a high cost problem. In order to avoid the α-case problem, expensive ceramics, such as CaO, ZrO2, and Y2O3 have been adopted as mold materials since the standard free energy changes of the formation of their oxides are more negative than TiO2. Regardless of thermodynamic approaches, the α-case formation reaction still remains to be eliminated in the chemical milling processes. Therefore, the exact α-case formation mechanism must be examined for practical applications of Ti–xAl alloys.

In this study, to examine the effects of Al contents on the α-case formation, we varied Al contents from 0 to 55at.%. Al2O3 was selected for the mold material because the standard free energy changes of the formation of the oxide are more negative than TiO2. In addition, Al2O3 features suitable strength, permeability, and collapse-ability, which can ensure dimensional accuracy of castings.

In order to prevent any contamination from refractory crucibles, Ti–xAl alloys were prepared using a non-consumable tungsten electrode plasma-arc melting (PAM) furnace equipped with a water-cooled copper crucible. Ti–xAl alloys compositions were pure Ti, Ti–10Al, Ti–30Al, Ti–40Al, Ti–47Al, and Ti–55at.% Al. The atmosphere in the PAM was controlled in the range 1.33×10−1 Pa by a rotary pump. In order to minimize the evaporation loss of Al in the Ti–xAl alloys, high purity argon was backfilled to the pressure of 4.9×103 Pa. To ensure the homogeneity of Ti–xAl alloys, the melting process was repeated five times.

The wax patterns for the examination of α-case reactions were made by pouring molten wax into a simple cylindrical silicon rubber mold (Ø 15mm×60mm). Subsequently, the patterns were inspected and dressed to eliminate any possible imperfection or contamination, and coated with Al2O3 slurry. The Al2O3 shell molds were dried at a controlled temperature (298±1K) and a relative humidity (40±1%) for 4h. Dipping, stuccoing, and drying procedures were repeated three times. After the primary layer coating, the patterns were coated with the back-up layers by the chamotte. To prevent shell cracks, the dewaxing process of the shell mold was carried out at around 423K and 0.5MPa in a steam autoclave. Finally, the shell molds were cured at 1223K for 2h.

The α-case formation reaction was examined in the PAM furnace with drop casting procedure as shown in Fig. 1 . After the drop casting, Ti castings were taken off the molds, sectioned, ground, polished, and etched using a Keller solution [10]. The microstructure in the α-case region of the castings was observed by Olympus PME3 microscopy, and its hardness was measured using a Mitutoyo MVK-H2 microvickers hardness tester with 100g load and 50μm intervals. For a closer examination of the α-case formation, the analysis of the elements distribution at the α-case region was performed by SHIMADZU EPMA 1600, and the phase structures of the reaction products were identified by JEOL JEM-3011 TEM.

Fig. 2 shows the surface microstructures of the Ti–xAl alloys investment castings with different Al concentrations poured into Al2O3 mold. When the Al concentrations of Ti–xAl alloys were below 30at.%, there were distinct α-case reaction layers on the interface. On the other hand, in the case of more than 30at.%, no α-case reaction occurred. These optical microstructure results of Ti–xAl alloys were in good accordance with the hardness profiles shown in Fig. 3 . In the Ti–xAl alloys casting, the α-case is generally known to be developed by the interstitials, such as carbon, nitrogen, and especially oxygen dissolved from mold materials. When the molten Ti (around 2000K) is poured into the Al2O3 investment mold, and if the α-case results between Ti and the interstitial oxygen, the reaction can be described as follows:
(1)

O 2

kJ / mol
(2)
4 3
Al ( l ) +
O 2
( g ) =

A
l 2
O 3
( s ) ,
Δ
G
A
l 2
O 3
° = − 1028.367
kJ / mol
(3)
Ti ( l ) +
2 3
A
l 2
O 3
( s ) = Ti

( s ) +
4 3
Al ( l ) ,
Δ G ° = + 99.748
kJ / mol
The calculations above utilized the joint army–navy–air force (JANAF) thermochemical tables [11]. According to Eq. (3), the formation of the α-case by interstitial oxygen cannot occur spontaneously. Therefore, the reason why the α-case reactions were generated below 30at.% Al concentration of Ti–xAl alloys cannot be explained by the conventional α-case formation mechanism.

For the clear examination of the α-case formation mechanism, the distribution of the elements on the α-case region of pure Ti castings and its chemical composition were investigated by EPMA elemental mapping as shown in Fig. 4 . In the α-case region, the oxygen element was uniformly distributed. Also, the Si element originating from the colloidal silica binder was scarcely detected on the surface. However, despite the pure Ti, a concentrated Al contamination layer about 30μm thick was detected on the interface. Until recently, the effect of substitutional metallic element dissolved from mold materials have been considered to be negligibly small [12]. However, Fig. 4 indicates that the effect of metallic elements cannot be overlooked any longer.

The phase identification of the detected Al element will surely be the very core of α-case formation mechanism. Accordingly, the phases of the detected Al on the interface were examined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). In order to observe α-case region precisely, the Ti castings specimen was ground from inside to the surface until about 30μm and final thinning was carried out using ion milling. Fig. 5(a) is the cross-sectional bright field TEM image of the α-case region. To examine the kind of phases which were composed in the α-case region, C2 aperture was temporarily removed. Fig. 5(b) shows ring and spot patterns on the TEM image without C2 aperture. The definite contrast of the continuous ring pattern could not be found on the TEM image since the ring pattern was extremely small size polycrystalline TiO2 phase. In the case of spot patterns in Fig. 5(c), the contrast could be distinguished on the TEM image. And the indexed pattern was hexagonal close-packed (HCP) phase in the [2ii0] beam direction as shown Fig. 5(c). The convergent beam electron diffraction (CBED) analysis was carried out for the verification of the phase of spot patterns by the primitive cell volume method. Primitive cell volume is characteristic material constant. So, the phase of interest can be easily identified by comparing the measured primitive cell volume of an unknown phase using CBED pattern with the known values of the possible phases [13,14]. This method is accurate (error range <10%) for the phase identification. In the CBED pattern where zero order Laue zone (ZOLZ) disk and high order Laue zone (HOLZ) ring are present, by measuring the distance of ZOLZ disk from the transmitted beam to the diffracted beam (D 1, D 2), an internal angle (ANG) between D 1 and D 2, and HOLZ ring's radius (CRAD), the primitive cell volume of unknown phase can be easily determined using Eq. (4). Camera length (CL) was calibrated with an Au standard sample at 200keV.
(4)
Cell
volume =
C
L 2
λ 3
D 1
D 2
sin ( ANG ) [ 1 − cos {
tan
− 1
( CRAD / CL ) } ]
Fig. 6(a) shows the CBED pattern of the HCP phase. There are two possible HCP phases of Ti and Ti3Al. The measured primitive cell volume (61.08ang3, CL=521.4mm, D 1 = D 2 =6.5mm, ANG=60°, CRAD=23mm) from CBED pattern corresponded to the theoretic value of Ti3Al (66.60ang3). This phase identification was in accordance with EDS spot analysis as shown in Fig. 6(b). The phase of the detected Al from EPMA mapping was Ti3Al phase. From the results of the microstructure, hardness profile, EPMA mapping, and TEM, it can be concluded that the α-case is not wholly TiO2, but TiO2 and Ti3Al between Ti and Al2O3 mold. We suggest, therefore, that the α-case is formed not only by interstitial oxygen element but also by substitutional metallic elements dissolved from mold materials.

Accordingly, the reason why no α-case reactions were generated in Ti–xAl alloys of more than 30at.% Al can be explained by our newly established α-case formation mechanism as below. First, more than 30at.% Ti–xAl alloys have lower melting points and negligibly low solubility. Thus, Ti–xAl alloys hardly form the interstitial α-case reaction since the incorporation of oxygen atoms into the Ti–xAl alloys castings are minimized. Secondly, the activity of molten Ti–xAl alloys of over 30at.% was reduced significantly to nearly one-tenth of that of molten pure Ti [15]. Moreover, the Ti–xAl alloys themselves were substitutional α-case reaction products. In the same manner, Ti–xAl alloys of more than 30at.% Al rarely form the substitutional α-case reaction.

Therefore, by the newly established α-case formation mechanism as above, it can be confirmed that the investment casting process is an economic net-shape technology for Ti–xAl alloys of more than 30at.% Al with an economic Al2O3 mold. Moreover, the clear and economic investment casting of below 30at.% Al concentration Ti–xAl alloys can be possible with α-case free mold materials based on Al2O3. In our study, α-case free mold materials were verified in the bases of ZrSiO4, ZrO2, and MgO as well as in Al2O3 base. Their detailed compositions and manufacturing processes will be further publicized in no distant future.

The reason why the α-case reactions were generated below 30at.% Al concentration of Ti–xAl alloys, and reversely why in the case of more than 30at.% Al concentration there were no α-case reactions cannot be explained by the conventional α-case formation mechanism. However, by combining the information derived from the microstructure, hardness profile, EPMA mapping, and TEM, we suggest that the α-case is formed not only by interstitial oxygen element but also by substitutional metallic elements dissolved from mold materials. Accordingly, the investment casting can be a net-shape forming for Ti–xAl alloys of more than 30at.% Al with an economic Al2O3 mold since there are neither interstitial nor substitutional α-case formations. Our newly established α-case formation mechanism will surely lead to a variety of significant applications of the α-case free Ti–xAl alloys casting.

sb:publisher.sb:name: American Chemical Society and American Institute of Physics for the National Bureau of Standards
